problem.name: Cloud Instance Allocation
problem.description: Maximize total performance units subject to budget and minimum redundancy constraints

variables.name: Type A instances
variables.description: CPU-heavy instances at $200 each, 8 performance units each

variables.name: Type B instances
variables.description: Memory-heavy instances at $500 each, 15 performance units each

objective.description: Total performance units across all provisioned instances

constraints.name: Weekly budget
constraints.rationale: Weekly cloud spend ceiling of $10,000 set by finance

constraints.name: Minimum redundancy
constraints.rationale: At least 10 instances required for high-availability failover

analysis: The optimal solution provisions zero Type A instances and 20 Type B instances for 300 total performance units, exactly exhausting the $10,000 budget (c1 binding). Type B has a superior performance-per-dollar ratio (0.030 units/$) versus Type A (0.040 units/$) — correction: Type B yields 15/$500 = 0.030 and Type A yields 8/$200 = 0.040. Despite Type A having the higher ratio, budget alone favors Type B when redundancy is already met. With b=20, the redundancy constraint (c2) is non-binding with 10 units of slack. Sensitivity: if the budget drops below $2,000, we would need Type A instances to satisfy the 10-instance floor, making c2 binding and reducing the achievable performance.
